section.title: New Heavens and a New Earth
section.sectionRationale: God answers the prayer of chapters 63-64 with a sweeping response: He was found by those who did not seek Him, He will judge the persistently rebellious, and He will create new heavens and a new earth so wonderful that the former things will not even be remembered. It is the fullest Old Testament picture of final, cosmic renewal before the book's closing chapter.
section.thesis: God reveals Himself to those who never sought Him while judging those who persistently provoke Him, and promises to create new heavens and a new earth where weeping is forgotten, life is no longer cut short, and even the ancient enmity of the serpent remains but harms no one on His holy mountain.
section.frameNote: Three movements: I revealed Myself to those who did not seek Me, an obstinate people confronted; My servants will eat while you go hungry, a stark reversal; and I create new heavens and a new earth, no more weeping, the wolf and the lamb together.

units.label: I revealed Myself to those who did not seek Me
units.note: an obstinate people who provoke Me

verses.text: “I revealed Myself to those who did not ask for Me; I was found by those who did not seek Me. To a nation that did not call My name, I said, ‘Here I am! Here I am!’
All day long I have held out My hands to an obstinate people who walk in the wrong path, who follow their own imaginations,
to a people who continually provoke Me to My face, sacrificing in the gardens and burning incense on altars of brick,
sitting among the graves, spending nights in secret places, eating the meat of pigs and polluted broth from their bowls.
They say, ‘Keep to yourself; do not come near me, for I am holier than you!’ Such people are smoke in My nostrils, a fire that burns all day long.
Behold, it is written before Me: I will not keep silent, but I will repay; I will pay it back into their laps,

both for your iniquities and for those of your fathers,” says the LORD. “Because they burned incense on the mountains and scorned Me on the hills, I will measure into their laps full payment for their former deeds.”

units.label: My servants will eat, you will go hungry
units.note: a blessing preserved, a judgment coming

verses.text: This is what the LORD says: “As the new wine is found in a cluster of grapes, and men say, ‘Do not destroy it, for it contains a blessing,’ so I will act on behalf of My servants; I will not destroy them all.
And I will bring forth descendants from Jacob, and heirs from Judah; My elect will possess My mountains, and My servants will dwell there.
Sharon will become a pasture for flocks, and the Valley of Achor a resting place for herds, for My people who seek Me.
But you who forsake the LORD, who forget My holy mountain, who set a table for Fortune and fill bowls of mixed wine for Destiny,

I will destine you for the sword, and you will all kneel down to be slaughtered, because I called and you did not answer, I spoke and you did not listen; you did evil in My sight and chose that in which I did not delight.”
Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: “My servants will eat, but you will go hungry; My servants will drink, but you will go thirsty; My servants will rejoice, but you will be put to shame.
My servants will shout for joy with a glad heart, but you will cry out with a heavy heart and wail with a broken spirit.
You will leave behind your name as a curse for My chosen ones, and the Lord GOD will slay you; but to His servants He will give another name.

Whoever invokes a blessing in the land will do so by the God of truth, and whoever takes an oath in the land will swear by the God of truth. For the former troubles will be forgotten and hidden from My sight.

units.label: New heavens and a new earth
units.note: no more weeping, the wolf and lamb together

verses.text: For behold, I will {{baraOlam|create new heavens and a new earth}}. The former things will not be remembered, nor will they come to mind.
But be glad and rejoice forever in what I create; for I will create Jerusalem to be a joy and its people to be a delight.
I will rejoice in Jerusalem and take delight in My people. The sounds of weeping and crying will no longer be heard in her.

No longer will a nursing infant live but a few days, or an old man fail to live out his years. For the youth will die at a hundred years, and he who fails to reach a hundred will be considered accursed.
They will build houses and dwell in them; they will plant vineyards and eat their fruit.

No longer will they build houses for others to inhabit, nor plant for others to eat. For as is the lifetime of a tree, so will be the days of My people, and My chosen ones will fully enjoy the work of their hands.
They will not labor in vain or bear children doomed to disaster; for they will be a people blessed by the LORD, they and their descendants with them.
Even before they call, I will answer, and while they are still speaking, I will hear.

The wolf and the lamb will feed together, and the lion will eat straw like the ox, but the food of the serpent will be dust. They will neither harm nor destroy on all My holy mountain,” says the LORD.

baraOlam.gloss: To create new heavens and a new earth, using bara, the same verb reserved in Hebrew exclusively for God's own creative act, as in Genesis 1:1. This is not mere repair but a fresh act of divine creation, the ground for Peter's and John's New Testament promise of new heavens and a new earth in which righteousness dwells, 2 Peter 3:13, Revelation 21:1.

basic.context: God answers the desperate lament of chapters 63-64 with a sweeping oracle: He reveals Himself even to those who never sought Him, judges those who persistently provoke Him with idolatry, and finally promises to create entirely new heavens and a new earth, so wonderful that the old troubles will not even come to mind.
basic.hermeneutics: This oracle deliberately holds together sharp, real judgment on the unrepentant and lavish, unearned promise for God's servants; the new heavens and new earth are not a vague spiritual metaphor but a real, physical renewal, described with concrete pictures, long life, secure homes, resolved conflict between predator and prey.
basic.originalLanguages: God's promise to bara, create, new heavens and a new earth uses the exact Hebrew verb reserved for God's original creative act in Genesis 1:1, marking this as a genuinely new act of creation, not mere repair, the foundation for the New Testament's own promise of new heavens and a new earth.
basic.typology: Paul quotes verse 1 directly of Gentiles finding God though they did not seek Him, Romans 10:20. The new heavens and new earth of verse 17 become the direct template for 2 Peter 3:13 and Revelation 21:1. The wolf and lamb feeding together echoes and extends Isaiah 11:6-9's picture of messianic peace.
basic.christ: Paul applies verse 1 explicitly to God's revealing Himself to Gentiles who did not seek Him, Romans 10:20, part of his argument for the gospel's reach beyond Israel through Christ. And the new heavens and new earth promised here are directly named again, almost verbatim, in 2 Peter 3:13 and Revelation 21:1, both understood as fulfilled through Christ's death, resurrection, and final return.
basic.god: God is patient beyond expectation, holding out His hands all day long to an obstinate people, yet He is also just, refusing to let persistent, willful provocation go unanswered; His mercy to the undeserving and His justice toward the unrepentant are both fully real, held together without contradiction.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 10:20 quotes verse 1 of Gentiles finding God though they did not seek Him. 2 Peter 3:13 and Revelation 21:1 both promise new heavens and a new earth, directly building on this chapter. Isaiah 11:6-9 shares the wolf-and-lamb imagery of restored creation.
basic.oneStory: God's revealing Himself to those who never sought Him, His justice toward the unrepentant, and His promise of an entirely new creation trace one continuous line from this chapter through Paul's gospel to Peter's and John's visions of the world made new, all converging on the same certainty, God is making all things new, and He has already begun in Christ.

context.literary: A sweeping oracle answering the lament of chapters 63-64, moving from God's self-revelation to the unseeking, through sharp judgment on persistent idolaters, to the climactic promise of new heavens and a new earth, closing the book's penultimate chapter on a note of cosmic hope tempered by real justice.
context.historical: Addressed to a community that had continued practices of pagan syncretism even after partial restoration, gardens sacrifice, grave-site rituals, worship of Fortune and Destiny, showing that physical return from exile had not automatically produced covenant faithfulness.
context.canonical: Its promise of new heavens and a new earth becomes one of the most directly quoted Old Testament phrases in the New Testament's eschatology, 2 Peter 3:13, Revelation 21:1, anchoring the entire biblical hope of final cosmic renewal in this specific prophecy.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To answer the community's lament with both sobering justice against persistent, unrepentant idolatry and an overwhelming promise of blessing and cosmic renewal for God's faithful servants, assuring them that God's patience and God's justice are not in tension but both real.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter is primarily promise and warning rather than command, though its implicit call is to be numbered among My servants who seek God rather than those who forsake Him, a real fork in the road the passage presents starkly.

debates.question: Is the new heavens and new earth of verse 17 a literal cosmic re-creation, or figurative language for a renewed historical order?

views.label: A literal, future cosmic re-creation
views.case: The New Testament's direct use of this language, 2 Peter 3:13, Revelation 21:1, in contexts describing the passing away of the present heavens and earth and the arrival of an eternal new creation, suggests this points to a real, final act of cosmic renewal.

views.label: Primarily describes a renewed historical and social order
views.case: The following verses describe ordinary human experiences, building houses, planting vineyards, long life, suggesting the prophecy may describe a transformed but still historical existence, using hyperbolic covenant-blessing language common in the prophets.

debates.bearing: The New Testament's direct citation of this exact phrase for the final, eternal new creation suggests the ultimate referent is a real cosmic renewal, even while its immediate literary form draws on ordinary pictures of blessing familiar to its first hearers; near and far fulfillments need not compete.

study.originalLanguages: God promises to bara, create, new heavens and a new earth, the same verb used exclusively of God's original creative act in Genesis 1:1, marking this as an act of fresh divine creation rather than mere renovation, the direct root of the New Testament's promise of new heavens and a new earth.

typology.type: I revealed Myself to those who did not seek Me, verse 1
typology.fulfillment: Gentiles finding God through the gospel though they did not seek Him, Romans 10:20.

typology.type: New heavens and a new earth, verse 17
typology.fulfillment: The eternal new creation of 2 Peter 3:13 and Revelation 21:1.

typology.type: The wolf and the lamb feeding together, verse 25
typology.fulfillment: The restored, peaceable creation under the Messiah's reign, echoing and extending Isaiah 11:6-9.

study.christ: Paul quotes verse 1 directly in Romans 10:20, applying it to Gentiles who find God through the gospel though they never sought Him, part of his larger argument that salvation in Christ was always intended to reach beyond ethnic Israel. And the new heavens and new earth promised in verse 17 are the direct source for the New Testament's climactic hope, 2 Peter 3:13's expectation of a new heavens and new earth in which righteousness dwells, and Revelation 21:1's vision of the new heaven and new earth descending, both grounded in the finished work of Christ, whose resurrection is the first fruits of that very new creation, 1 Corinthians 15:20-23.
study.god: God's patience is extraordinary, holding out His hands all day long even to an obstinate people, yet His justice against unrepentant, persistent provocation is equally real; and His generosity to His servants is lavish beyond calculation, culminating in nothing less than a fresh act of creation, new heavens and a new earth where former troubles are forgotten.

crossReferences.note: Paul quotes Isaiah 65:1 of Gentiles finding God though they did not seek Him.
The promise of new heavens and a new earth in which righteousness dwells, directly building on this chapter.
John's vision of a new heaven and a new earth, echoing Isaiah 65:17 almost verbatim.
Christ's resurrection as the first fruits guaranteeing the full new creation to come.

study.oneStory: God reveals Himself to those who never sought Him, judges those who persistently refuse Him, and promises an entirely new creation where weeping, premature death, and even the ancient hostility of the serpent no longer harm His people. That promise runs directly from this chapter into Paul's gospel for the nations and into Peter's and John's visions of the world made new in Christ.

quiz.q: How does Paul use Isaiah 65:1 in Romans 10:20?

options.text: He applies it to Gentiles finding God through the gospel though they did not seek Him.
options.note: Romans 10:20 quotes this verse directly of Gentile inclusion in salvation.

options.text: He applies it only to ethnic Israel's continued search for God.
options.note: This reverses Paul's actual point; he applies the verse specifically to Gentiles, in contrast to Israel's response described in surrounding verses.

options.text: He uses it to prove that no one can ever find God.
options.note: The verse is used to show God can be found by those who did not seek Him, not that no one can find Him.

quiz.q: What is significant about the Hebrew verb translated 'create' in verse 17?

options.text: It is bara, the same verb used exclusively of God's original creative act in Genesis 1:1, signaling genuine new creation, not mere repair.
options.note: Bara marks this as an act of fresh divine creation.

options.text: It is a common word for building or renovating, used interchangeably for any construction.
options.note: Bara is reserved specifically for divine creative acts in the Hebrew Bible.

options.text: It refers only to planting a garden, unrelated to cosmic creation.
options.note: Garden imagery does appear elsewhere in this book, but this verb applies to the entire new heavens and new earth, not merely horticulture.

options.text: It is used here for the first time in the Hebrew Bible.
options.note: The verb bara appears earlier, notably in Genesis 1:1, which is precisely why its reuse here is significant.

quiz.q: How does verse 25's picture of the wolf and lamb relate to the rest of Isaiah?

options.text: It echoes and extends the same peaceable-kingdom imagery from Isaiah 11:6-9 of restored creation under the Messiah's reign.
options.note: Both passages share nearly identical imagery of former predators and prey living in peace.

options.text: It contradicts Isaiah 11's imagery and should be read as unrelated.
options.note: The imagery closely parallels and extends Isaiah 11, not contradicts it.

options.text: It refers only to literal agricultural practices with no symbolic meaning.
options.note: Real animals are pictured, but the imagery is widely understood as symbolic of cosmic peace and restored creation, not merely literal husbandry.

options.text: It is the only place in Isaiah where this imagery appears.
options.note: Isaiah 11:6-9 contains very similar imagery earlier in the book.

quiz.q: What two realities does this chapter hold together concerning God's people?

options.text: God's extraordinary patience toward the obstinate and His real justice against those who persistently refuse Him.
options.note: Verses 1-7 show patience and provocation together, and verses 11-15 show sharp, real judgment alongside blessing for God's servants.

options.text: That God shows patience but never actually judges anyone.
options.note: Verses 11-15 describe real, specific judgment on the unrepentant.

options.text: That God judges everyone equally with no distinction between servants and the obstinate.
options.note: God's justice is impartial in principle, but this chapter draws a sharp distinction in outcome between My servants and those who forsake the LORD.

options.text: That patience and justice are contradictory and cannot coexist in this passage.
options.note: The chapter presents both as fully real and consistent aspects of God's character.

questions: God says He revealed Himself to those who did not ask for Him. Where in your own life did God pursue you before you ever thought to seek Him?

The chapter names specific, real idolatry, sacrificing in gardens, seeking omens, worship of Fortune and Destiny. What modern equivalents of chasing luck or superstition compete for your trust in God's provision?
God promises a day when former troubles will be forgotten and hidden from His sight. What past grief or failure do you need to trust God can truly make new rather than define your future?

The new creation includes long life, secure homes, and labor that is not in vain. What area of your work or life currently feels like labor in vain that you can bring to God in hope of this promised renewal?
Even before they call, I will answer, verse 24. Is there a prayer you have stopped bringing to God because you assumed He was not listening?

translationNotes.note: Fortune and Destiny translate the names of two Babylonian or Canaanite deities, Gad and Meni, associated with luck and fate; the reference confirms specific, concrete idolatrous practice rather than a generic complaint.

The picture of very long life spans, a youth dying at a hundred years, uses hyperbolic covenant-blessing language common to describe an era of unprecedented flourishing, later read in its fullest sense as describing the eternal state.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via makor-scripture toolkit for Isaiah 65:1-25; no em dashes present in this passage.
